The Transfer Portal in Women's College Basketball

By Samantha Archie

Introduction

The transfer portal is changing the landscape of women's basketball. What was once a rare event is now much more widespread, but it is unclear what impact these trends are having on the game as a whole. Does utilizing transfers increase the likelihood of succeeding? Are players who transfer more successful at their next school? What types of players transfer to what types of programs?

This article is Part 1 of a multi-part series examining the effect of the transfer portal on women’s college basketball. This article will focus on providing background information about the transfer portal, descriptive statistics on transfer patterns, and an analysis on whether utilizing transfers increases the likelihood of succeeding in the NCAA tournament.

Transfer Portal Background

The timeline below documents key policy changes related to transferring in college sports.

2018

Transfer Portal Introduced

The policy formalized the transfer process by creating a system where athletes can register and then be formally contacted by potential programs.

2020

COVID-19 One-Year Eligibility Extension

Athletes on rosters during 2020–2021 received an extra year of eligibility due to disruptions from the COVID-19 pandemic.

2021

One-Time Transfer with Immediate Eligibility

Athletes can transfer once and play immediately, unlike before when they had to sit out one year at their new school.

2024

Elimination of Transfer Limits

Athletes can now transfer multiple times and play immediately at their new school with no restrictions.

Throughout the remainder of this article and series, the “transfer portal” will refer to the 2021 policy change that allowed players to transfer without having to sit out a year.

Data

The data for this analysis come from wehoop, which is an R package that provides play-by-play and box score data for women’s college and professional basketball. The analysis was conducted in python. Data are from the 2014-2026 seasons. 2014 is the season when box score data for many of the regular season games for most schools became available. The data are organized as one row per player per season.

To calculate the transfer status, for each athlete I compared the previous season’s team with the current season’s team and when they were different, I counted them as a transfer.

The analyses below fall into two categories each with a different dataset:

  • League-wide trends (the annual number of transfers, transfer rate, etc.). These analyses are calculated using data from the 2015-2026 seasons. The 2014 season is used as a baseline to determine transfer status.
  • Class-level trends (i.e. number of transfers per athlete over their career). These analyses are also calculated using data from the 2015-2026 seasons. However, they use a cohort dataset where players who appear in the data during a given season, but who do not appear in a prior season are noted as being a member of that season’s cohort. These cohort assignments start with the 2015 season (using the 2014 season to identify who appears for the first time). Analyses include up to the 2023 season cohort to allow players to have had the opportunity to play at least four years of college basketball.

Analysis

The number of transfers and the transfer rate has increased substantially since the start of the transfer portal

Prior to the transfer portal, transferring was a relatively rare event, with only 2-5% of players transferring in a given season. However, as shown in the graph below, in the season following the advent of the transfer portal, both the number of transfers and the transfer rate nearly doubles from 326 to 638 and 6.8% to 12.2%, respectively. Those statistics nearly double again from 2022 to 2026 (638-1010, 12.2%-21.8%).

How many times do athletes transfer during their career?

The chart below shows the number of transfers by the player’s first season in college. Most players remain at the school they first enrolled in. However, transfer rates start increasing with the 2018 freshmen, which aligns with the COVID-19 policy change that gave an extra year of eligibility due if they were on a team during the 2021 season. The 2021 freshmen had the highest transfer rates as they benefited from both the extra year of eligibility and the transfer portal being available to them after their freshman season. Rates have begun to shrink again in recent year, but not substantially.

Are teams with more transfers more successful?

There has been debate about whether exploiting the transfer portal is an effective strategy to win championships. This visualization below shows the share of transfers among starters on the five most recent championship teams, with the orange jerseys representing transfers. The data suggests that teams with championship pedigrees, like UConn and South Carolina, utilize transfers less than relative newcomers, like UCLA and LSU.

Transfer usage among Elite 8 teams

When the window is extended to the top eight teams from the past five tournaments, transferring does seem to help teams compete for championships much quicker than before, as shown in this graph which plots Elite 8 appearances against the share of minutes played by transfers. Overall, the results show a negative relationship between historic success and transfer utilization, which supports the findings of the championship figure above. The red and blue dots represent UConn and South Carolina, the two of the most dominant programs in recent years. Their teams have between 0 – 30% of their minutes played by transfers. On the other hand, nearly one third of the teams have over 40% of their minutes played by transfers, including recent champions LSU and UCLA. While these results are not causal, they do suggest that the transfer portal may be helping increase parity in a sport that has been historically dominated by a few programs.

What about Name, Image, and Likeness (NIL)?

An important thing to note is that the transfer portal changes occurred at the same time as another important change in college sports: name, image, and likeness (NIL). As a result of lawsuits brought by former collegiate athletes, since 2021, the NCAA allows athletes to earn compensation beyond college tuition. NIL contracts organizations by colleges and/or their affiliated collective groups have been used as a recruiting tool. Athletes who have proven to be successful at the collegiate level have garnered large transfer NIL packages (Espinoza, 2026). More discussion on this topic will come in another article in this series.

Conclusion

The transfer portal is changing the landscape of women's basketball. What was once a rare event is now much more widespread. NCAA policy changes have facilitated an increase in transfers. While some of the increase is related to the extra year of eligibility from COVID-19, transfer trends are persisting beyond that window. Programs are utilizing the transfer portal to build competitive teams much faster than that the previous norm.

Below is a list of future topics that this series plans to explore:

  • Do players who transfer enjoy more success at their new school?
  • Why do players transfer?
  • What types of players transfer?
  • How different are transfer patterns for power and non-power conference schools?
  • What types of programs are exploiting the transfer portal?
  • How is name, image, and likeness (NIIL) influencing transfer decisions?
